cases.title: Lymph node excision: reactive follicular hyperplasia
cases.clinicalHistory: 24-year-old woman with tender cervical lymphadenopathy after an upper respiratory infection; flow cytometry shows no monotypic B-cell or aberrant T-cell population.

image.caption: Reactive follicular hyperplasia with polarized germinal centers and preserved lymph node compartments.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: excisional 1st biopsy from cervical lymph node a and b low power view reactive appearing follicles with preserved architecture c infarcted lymph node with occasional large cells with prominent nucleoli on high magnification d cd20 e cd3 f bcl 2 g bcl 6 h eber and lmp 1

diagnosticSteps: Assess architecture at low power for preserved capsule, sinuses, paracortex, and variably sized follicles rather than back-to-back monotonous nodules.
Confirm reactive follicle features including polarization, tangible-body macrophages, and intact mantle zones.
Correlate with flow cytometry and immunostains to exclude follicular lymphoma or another clonal lymphoid process.

ancillaryOptions.name: CD20 and CD3
ancillaryOptions.result: Preserved B-cell follicles and T-cell paracortex support reactive architecture.

ancillaryOptions.name: BCL2, CD10, and BCL6
ancillaryOptions.result: Reactive germinal centers are CD10/BCL6 positive and BCL2 negative.

ancillaryOptions.name: IGH clonality assay
ancillaryOptions.result: Not required when morphology, immunophenotype, and flow cytometry are reactive.

synopticChecklist: evidence of lymphoma absent or present
cases.reportingTarget: Lymph node, cervical, excision: Reactive follicular hyperplasia. No morphologic or immunophenotypic evidence of lymphoma.
pitfalls: Do not call follicular lymphoma solely because follicles are numerous.
Do not ignore flow cytometry limitations if only a small or necrotic portion was submitted.
Do not omit comment on architecture in an excisional lymph node specimen.

cases.title: Lymph node core biopsy: follicular lymphoma, low grade
cases.clinicalHistory: 62-year-old man with slowly progressive inguinal and retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy; PET/CT shows low to moderate FDG uptake.

image.caption: Follicular lymphoma with closely packed atypical follicles on H&E stain.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: entities causing potential fp diagnoses a kfl with increased numbers of activated large lymphocytes superficially mimicking lymphoma with large cell morphology hemacolor 40 b kfl showing prominent crescentic histiocytes pap 60 c pmx showing seemingly crowded sheets of basaloid cells with hyperchromatic nuclei pap 40 d pmx showing nucleated epithelial cells transitioning to ghost cells pap 40 e wt featuring atypical orangeophilic cells closely mimicking keratinising scc pap 20 f metastatic scc showing diagnostic crowded disorganised sheets of cytologically malignant cells pap 20

diagnosticSteps: Recognize an atypical follicular proliferation with effaced architecture, crowded follicles, attenuated mantle zones, and reduced polarization.
Grade the sampled lymphoma by estimating centroblasts per high-power field while noting that limited core tissue may under-sample grade 3 or transformation.
Integrate immunophenotype and flow cytometry showing a germinal-center B-cell population with light-chain restriction and aberrant BCL2 expression.

ancillaryOptions.name: CD20, CD10, BCL6, and BCL2
ancillaryOptions.result: Neoplastic follicles are CD20 positive with CD10/BCL6 expression and aberrant BCL2 positivity.

ancillaryOptions.name: Ki-67
ancillaryOptions.result: Low proliferation index supports low-grade follicular lymphoma in the sampled tissue.

ancillaryOptions.name: FISH for IGH::BCL2
ancillaryOptions.result: May support the diagnosis when immunophenotype is atypical or tissue is limited.

cases.reportingTarget: Lymph node, inguinal, core biopsy: Follicular lymphoma, classic type, low grade (grade 1-2 of 3) in the sampled tissue. No diffuse large B-cell lymphoma identified in this biopsy.
requiredReportElements: no diffuse large B-cell lymphoma identified
pitfalls: Do not overstate complete nodal architecture on a core biopsy.
Do not exclude transformation if PET-avid disease was not sampled.
Do not rely on BCL2 alone because reactive follicles and variant follicular lymphomas can be misleading.

cases.title: Lymph node excision: diffuse large B-cell lymphoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 71-year-old man with rapidly enlarging axillary lymphadenopathy, B symptoms, and LDH elevation.

image.caption: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma composed of sheets of large atypical lymphoid cells.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: histological findings of the inguinal lymph node excision 2 weeks after initial presentation a low power view 25 shows a lymph node with a preserved architecture with intact capsule patent sinuses and polarized germinal centers with well demarcated mantle zones the interfollicular areas are expanded h e b at higher magnification 100 the interfollicular infiltrate is composed of numerous large transformed immunoblasts in the background of small lymphocytes abundant eosinophils scattered plasma cells and histiocytes h e c at 200 magnification h e and d at 400 magnification the large atypical cells have large oval to slightly irregular nuclei with vesicular chromatin prominent nucleoli and scant to moderate amounts of clear to amphophilic cytoplasm h e e the large atypical cells are immunoreactive for cd20 cd20 immunostain 50 as well as for f pax5 pax5 immunostain 200 g cd30 cd30 immunostain 200 and h mum1 mum1 immunostain 200

diagnosticSteps: Identify diffuse architectural effacement by sheets of large atypical lymphoid cells with vesicular chromatin and prominent nucleoli.
Confirm B-cell lineage and classify cell-of-origin by an accepted immunohistochemical algorithm when molecular profiling is not performed.
Evaluate MYC, BCL2, and BCL6 protein expression and order cytogenetic testing when high-grade B-cell lymphoma with rearrangements is a concern.

ancillaryOptions.name: CD20, PAX5, and CD3
ancillaryOptions.result: Large atypical cells are CD20/PAX5 positive and CD3 negative, supporting B-cell lineage.

ancillaryOptions.name: MYC, BCL2, and BCL6 FISH
ancillaryOptions.result: Recommended to exclude high-grade B-cell lymphoma with MYC and BCL2 and/or BCL6 rearrangements.

ancillaryOptions.name: EBER in situ hybridization
ancillaryOptions.result: Useful in older or immunocompromised patients and selected morphologic settings.

cases.reportingTarget: Lymph node, axillary, excision: Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, NOS, germinal center B-cell phenotype by Hans algorithm. MYC/BCL2 double-expressor status and MYC/BCL2/BCL6 FISH pending.
pitfalls: Do not sign out DLBCL without addressing high-grade B-cell lymphoma with rearrangements when MYC is expressed or morphology is high grade.
Do not miss an underlying low-grade lymphoma component.
Do not delay urgent communication when the diagnosis has immediate treatment implications.

cases.title: Lymph node excision: classic Hodgkin lymphoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 33-year-old woman with mediastinal mass, pruritus, and contiguous cervical lymphadenopathy.

image.caption: Classic Hodgkin lymphoma with scattered Reed-Sternberg cells in a mixed inflammatory background.

diagnosticSteps: Find diagnostic Hodgkin/Reed-Sternberg cells in the appropriate polymorphous inflammatory background rather than interpreting scattered activated immunoblasts as lymphoma.
Confirm the classic Hodgkin immunophenotype and document weak PAX5 with CD30 and CD15 expression.
Classify the subtype when possible and exclude nodular lymphocyte-predominant Hodgkin lymphoma and mediastinal gray-zone lymphoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: CD30, CD15, and PAX5
ancillaryOptions.result: Hodgkin cells are CD30 positive, usually CD15 positive, and show weak PAX5 expression.

ancillaryOptions.name: CD45, CD20, OCT2, and BOB1
ancillaryOptions.result: Pattern helps separate classic Hodgkin lymphoma from mimics including NLPHL and large B-cell lymphoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: Flow cytometry
ancillaryOptions.result: Often non-diagnostic in classic Hodgkin lymphoma and should not override tissue morphology.

cases.reportingTarget: Lymph node, mediastinal, excision: Classic Hodgkin lymphoma, nodular sclerosis type. EBER pending.
pitfalls: Do not exclude classic Hodgkin lymphoma because flow cytometry is negative.
Do not confuse NLPHL with lymphocyte-rich classic Hodgkin lymphoma.
Do not under-sample mediastinal lesions where gray-zone lymphoma is in the differential.

cases.title: Lymph node core biopsy: mantle cell lymphoma with flow correlation
cases.specimenType: Image-guided lymph node core biopsy with concurrent flow cytometry
cases.clinicalHistory: 68-year-old man with leukocytosis, splenomegaly, and multiple mesenteric lymph nodes; flow cytometry detects a CD5-positive, kappa-restricted B-cell population that is CD23 negative.

image.caption: Mantle cell lymphoma with nuclear cyclin D1 expression.

diagnosticSteps: Correlate the flow cytometry phenotype of monotypic CD5-positive/CD23-negative B cells with tissue involvement by a small to intermediate lymphoid infiltrate.
Confirm mantle cell lymphoma by cyclin D1 or SOX11 expression and/or CCND1 rearrangement.
Assess blastoid or pleomorphic morphology and proliferation index because these features change risk assessment.

ancillaryOptions.name: Cyclin D1 and SOX11
ancillaryOptions.result: Nuclear cyclin D1 expression with SOX11 positivity supports mantle cell lymphoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: FISH for CCND1::IGH
ancillaryOptions.result: Confirms t(11;14) when immunostains are equivocal or cyclin D1-negative mantle cell lymphoma is considered.

ancillaryOptions.name: TP53 sequencing or p53 immunostain
ancillaryOptions.result: Useful for prognostic workup but not required for the initial tissue diagnosis in every setting.

cases.reportingTarget: Lymph node, mesenteric, core biopsy: Mantle cell lymphoma, classic morphology, involving core biopsy tissue. Ki-67 proliferation index approximately 20%. Concurrent flow cytometry shows a CD5-positive, CD23-negative, kappa-restricted B-cell population.
requiredReportElements: cyclin D1/SOX11 or CCND1 rearrangement status
pitfalls: Do not diagnose CLL/SLL solely because the lymphoma is CD5 positive.
Do not omit Ki-67 in mantle cell lymphoma.
Do not miss blastoid morphology in a small core.

cases.title: Bone marrow biopsy: acute myeloid leukemia
cases.specimenType: Bone marrow aspirate, clot section, and core biopsy
cases.clinicalHistory: 59-year-old man with fatigue, circulating blasts, hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL, platelets 34 K/uL, and absolute neutropenia.

image.caption: Acute myeloid leukemia with increased blasts on bone marrow aspirate smear.

sourceArticle.citation: Cold Spring Harb Mol Case Stud. 2021 Jun; 7(3):a006084
sourceArticle.figureCaption: a hypercellular bone marrow biopsy showing increased immature cells and granulocytic predominance hematoxylin and eosin h e 400 b bone marrow touch imprint showing increased blasts and left shifted granulocytes with some dysplastic features wright giemsa 1000 oil immersion

diagnosticSteps: Quantify blasts on aspirate differential and core biopsy, then determine whether AML-defining genetic abnormalities or blast threshold criteria are met.
Use flow cytometry to establish myeloid lineage and define aberrant antigen expression useful for measurable residual disease planning.
Order cytogenetic and molecular studies needed for WHO/ICC classification and risk stratification before final integrated classification.

ancillaryOptions.name: Acute leukemia flow cytometry panel
ancillaryOptions.result: Expanded blast population expresses CD34, CD117, CD13, CD33, MPO, and aberrant CD7.

ancillaryOptions.name: Conventional karyotype and AML FISH
ancillaryOptions.result: Required to identify recurrent genetic abnormalities and therapy-defining lesions.

ancillaryOptions.name: Myeloid NGS panel
ancillaryOptions.result: Recommended for classification refinement and prognostic/risk assignment.

cases.reportingTarget: Bone marrow, aspirate, clot section, and core biopsy: Acute myeloid leukemia with approximately 62% blasts by aspirate differential. Flow cytometry confirms an aberrant myeloid blast population. Cytogenetic and molecular studies pending for final subclassification.
pitfalls: Do not finalize AML subclassification before required genetic data if it will change the diagnostic category.
Do not rely on core CD34 alone when aspirate and flow data are available.
Do not miss acute promyelocytic leukemia when abnormal promyelocytes or coagulopathy are present.

cases.title: Bone marrow biopsy: B-lymphoblastic leukemia by flow cytometry
cases.specimenType: Bone marrow aspirate and core biopsy with flow cytometry
cases.clinicalHistory: 17-year-old male with bruising, bone pain, WBC 46 K/uL, anemia, thrombocytopenia, and 72% circulating blasts.

image.caption: Bone marrow involved by acute lymphoblastic leukemia with sheets of blasts.

diagnosticSteps: Confirm marrow replacement by blasts and correlate morphology with flow cytometry showing precursor B-cell immunophenotype.
Document lineage-defining markers including TdT and B-lineage antigens while excluding mixed-phenotype acute leukemia criteria.
Order genetic studies for recurrent B-ALL abnormalities because BCR::ABL1, KMT2A rearrangement, ETV6::RUNX1, hyperdiploidy, and Ph-like features affect therapy.

ancillaryOptions.name: B-ALL flow cytometry panel
ancillaryOptions.result: Blasts express CD19, CD10, TdT, CD34, CD38, CD22, and dim CD45 with absent surface light chains.

ancillaryOptions.name: BCR::ABL1 testing
ancillaryOptions.result: Required to identify Philadelphia chromosome-positive B-ALL.

ancillaryOptions.name: Karyotype, B-ALL FISH, and molecular testing
ancillaryOptions.result: Recommended for classification, risk stratification, and clinical-trial eligibility.

cases.reportingTarget: Bone marrow, aspirate and core biopsy: B-lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma involving hypercellular marrow, with approximately 88% blasts by flow cytometry. BCR::ABL1 and additional cytogenetic/molecular studies pending.
pitfalls: Do not confuse hematogones with B-ALL; evaluate maturation spectrum and aberrancy.
Do not omit mixed-phenotype acute leukemia assessment when myeloid markers are expressed.
Do not delay BCR::ABL1 testing in a new B-ALL diagnosis.

cases.title: Gross-only specimen: benign thymic tissue from cardiac surgery
cases.clinicalHistory: 5-month-old infant undergoing congenital cardiac repair; small fragment of grossly unremarkable thymic tissue submitted from surgical exposure.

image.caption: Grossly unremarkable thymic tissue may be submitted incidentally during pediatric cardiothoracic surgery.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: cynomolgus ovary teratoma benign showing bone hair and connective tissue h e high magnification modified from cline et al 2008

diagnosticSteps: Confirm the specimen is clinically incidental thymic tissue from an infant or child and not a targeted mass excision.
Inspect for nodules, firmness, hemorrhage, necrosis, or other gross abnormality that would require microscopic submission.
Apply local gross-only policy and document weight, dimensions, color, consistency, and absence of gross lesions.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: No ancillary study is indicated for grossly unremarkable incidental thymic tissue.

ancillaryOptions.name: Representative histologic section
ancillaryOptions.result: Submit tissue for microscopy if there is a gross lesion, atypical clinical history, or local policy requires histology.

ancillaryOptions.name: Flow cytometry
ancillaryOptions.result: Not indicated without suspicion for lymphoma or leukemia involvement.

cases.reportingTarget: Thymus, incidental excision: Grossly unremarkable thymic tissue, gross examination only.
pitfalls: Do not use gross-only handling for an adult anterior mediastinal mass.
Do not discard tissue with nodules, necrosis, or a concerning clinical history.
Do not omit that the diagnosis is based on gross examination only.
